A three-year starter at Cal Poly, Meyer's glove also earned him a spot on USA Baseball's Collegiate National Team. He has excellent hands and moves well behind the plate, allowing him to receive and block well. He has a strong and accurate arm, recording excellent pop times to second base on a consistent basis. Offensively, the right-handed hitter has been less productive. He does make consistent contact and rarely strikes out with decent on-base skills. And he's having his best offensive season now, albeit without any power. Meyer more than likely profiles as a backup at the next level. If his bat comes around with pro instruction, he could become a defensive-minded regular. In either case, his defensive skillset should have him off the board in the top six rounds.

Moritz won Southern Conference batting titles in his first two seasons with a .400 average as a freshman and a .392 mark as a sophomore. He's well on his way to a third, ranking second in NCAA Division I at .421 at the end of the regular season. As one of the best pure hitters in college baseball, he could surpass Jermaine Mitchell (fifth round to the Athletics in 2006) as the highest-drafted position player in UNC Greensboro history. Moritz possesses a pretty left-handed stroke and uncanny feel for the barrel. With a contact-oriented approach, he easily sprays line drives all over the field. He doesn't have much over-the-fence power but does have a knack for slashing triples. With solid speed and keen instincts that allow him to play even quicker on the bases and in the field, Moritz offers value beyond his bat. He has a good chance to stay in center field at the next level, a key to him profiling as a regular. If he can't, he has enough arm strength to play all three outfield spots and serve as a sweet-swinging reserve.

A Rawlings-Perfect Game Honorable Mention All-American for 2018, Butler is a 6-foot-4, left-handed-hitting first baseman with plus raw power, although he could work on his approach at the plate. The West Virginia commit is athletic and a solid runner, but raw defensively. He does potentially have the arm to play left field.

There are college pitchers who are labeled as "performers," those whose numbers are better than their pure stuff would indicate they're capable of. Then there are hurlers whose stuff is actually better than the stats they put up. Flynn, who served as Arizona's Saturday starter in 2018, fits into the second category. Flynn has four pitches, all of which he is capable of throwing for strikes. He'll throw his fastball in the 90-94 mph range and he'll back it up with a pair of breaking balls. He'll throw a curve 79-82 mph and will also mix in a mid-80s slider that has cutter-like action to it. His 83-85 mph changeup gives him a fourth at least Major League average offering. While his command hasn't been pinpoint, he is generally around the strike zone. Flynn's ceiling is that of a No. 4 or 5 starter at the next level, though he did put up better numbers largely as a reliever as a sophomore. For him to go in the top five or so rounds, teams will have to look past his numbers and have a feel that his stuff will play in a professional rotation.

King High School has had its fair share of high Draft picks, dating back to former big leaguer Derek Bell, a second-rounder in 1987 and up to 2012 supplemental first-round pick Keon Barnum. Barger doesn't look like a top-two-round guy at this point, but his skills on both sides of the ball could land him in the top five. Barger is a left-handed hitting middle infielder who has shown some plus bat speed at times this spring. It's more of a line-drive approach at present, but there is raw power for him to tap into as he progresses in the game. Defensively, Barger has a strong arm and good hands, though with fringy speed, at best, the question remains whether he can stick at shortstop long-term. Scouts think Barger can begin his career at the premium position and let him play his way off, with second or third a more likely landing place. Seeing Barger end up as an offensive-minded second baseman from the left side of the plate might be the best scenario, and he could profile best as a super-utility type who can hit. There is some offensive upside, and that could be enough to entice a team to sign him away from his Florida commitment.

Chandler led his junior college pitching staff at Iowa Western, going 11-0 with a 1.34 ERA and 123 strikeouts in 74 innings, including five complete games. Pitching out of a high three-quarters delivery, the 6-foot-5, 200-pound right-hander will sit 91-to-93 mph with his fastball and touch 95, and he'll spin some curveballs. Chandler hides the ball well, and he's been improving.

Drafted by the Brewers in the 32nd round last year, Hogan has a high floor, with a 93 mph four-seamer and a high-80-mph two-seamer that is actually a better pitch for him. The right-hander also features a changeup and curveball, and has a strong track record of success.

Haake has impressive stuff, especially when he pitches out of the bullpen, which is where he might end up in pro ball. The 6-foot-4 righty can hit 97 mph with his fastball and couples it with an 85-to-86 mph plus slider, although he doesn't use his changeup much. But he's had some issues this season, when he moved between Kentucky's bullpen and rotation. Haake had an 8.47 ERA in 34 innings, although he did strike out 36 batters. He doesn't repeat his delivery consistently, which affects his ability to throw strikes.

There are three players from New York in MLB Pipeline's Draft Top 200 this year. Mack is the lone hitter of the trio, with enough thump in the bat to interest teams in the top five or six rounds, albeit without a sure-fire defensive home. Mack is stocky and physical at 5-foot-11, but athletic enough to play shortstop capably for his high school team. Few see him sticking at the position, however, because he's a bit heavy-legged in terms of his range and while he does have some arm strength, he doesn't always show the ability to throw from different arm angles, a skill needed for the premium position. A move to second might work, with others suggesting third or left field, where his arm would probably play up to average. Playing a corner spot would put more pressure on his bat, and there is some serious raw power for him to tap into. That power, and the hope he'll hit enough to get to it consistently, is what teams will be banking on should they take the left-handed hitter. Whether a team likes it enough to lure him away from his Clemson commitment remains to be seen.

Rasmussen had been a big part of Oregon State's pitching staff as a freshman in 2015 and at the start of the 2016 season, though he needed Tommy John surgery in March of that year. After returning in late April in 2017, and pitching well in short stints, the Rays took the right-hander with the 31st overall pick in the Draft. Citing discrepencies between the pre-Draft and post-Draft MRIs, the Rays didn't sign Rasmussen. He was set to return to the Beavers' pitching staff when he required a second TJ surgery in September, missing the entire season. At this point, it's hard to know what to expect from Rasmussen when he does come back. In all likelihood, he'd be viewed as a reliever only given his medical history, and his stuff did look good a year ago in those shorter outings. He was showing a fastball in the 92-96 mph range in his limited time back, thrown with good extension and getting swings and misses with it. He was getting back to average feel of his slider rand changeup the more he threw during OSU's postseason run a year ago. Rasmussen does have three effective pitches and a starter's delivery, not to mention some track record of success in that role. But after two elbow surgeries, his future almost certainly has to be in a bullpen, where his stuff does play up.

A three-year starter at Loyola Marymount, Decolati's strong showing in the Cape Cod League last summer had many excited to see what he'd do during his junior season. While the raw tools are there, his performance hasn't measured up, making it a little tougher to place him on Draft boards. A product of the Las Vegas high school ranks, Decolati has a lot of potential weapons he'll bring to the pro game. The right-handed hitter has a ton of bat speed and is capable of making very loud contact. He has outstanding raw power, but his aggressive approach has led to swing-and-miss issues that have hurt his performance this spring, which makes some wonder if he'll hit enough to reach that power consistently. Defensively, Decolati has a strong arm and will show the ability to make tough plays, but will boot routine ones at times. Primarily a shortstop in college, he might be better suited for third base, with some thinking a corner outfield spot would work. It's possible Decolati ends up filling a super-utilty role in the future. How much his bat can develop will determine his ultimate job and the team thinking they can help him tap into his offensive potential will be the one to take him on Day 2 of the Draft.

Granberg has hit throughout his collegiate career, hitting .427/.508/.680 with 25 home runs in 163 games between Creighton, Cisco Junior College and Dallas Baptist. But he has never been drafted to this point despite the strong offensive production. The outfielder has good hand-eye coordination at the plate and generates good exit velocity. He can also run for a player of his size.

Delgado made some noise over the summer on the showcase circuit, playing well enough to make USA Baseball's 18 and Under team that won gold. He's shown some promise with the bat this spring at Calvary Christian Academy, but has been more inconsistent than some scouts had hoped. Delgado's carrying tool is his bat. A switch-hitter, he has shown a feel to hit from both sides, and his bat speed should give him the chance to hit for average and power as he progresses. There are more questions about his defense. A shortstop in high school, most feel he won't stay there because his below-average speed limits his range. He's struggled with the accuracy of his throws this spring, making it tougher to project where his long-term defensive home will be, though he has shown enough arm strength in the past to make some believe he could play third. Otherwise, a future as an offensive-minded second baseman might make more sense. Any team considering Delgado in the top five or so rounds will be doing so because of the bat and the hopes of figuring out his position down the road. Whether or not he can be signed away from his Florida International commitment remains to be seen.

Smith led the Bulldogs with 76 strikeouts this season in 59 2/3 innings. The 6-foot-5, 233-pound left-hander has been sitting 88-to-92 mph with his fastball, although he got it up to 94 in the Cape Cod League. Smith has a very good sweeping slider that he can work to both sides of the plate, and his changeup is coming along. That stuff helps him eat up lefties.

A two-sport standout who has played both wide receiver and safety for the Rutgers football team, Harris has been a three-year starter on the baseball field as well, giving scouts a toolsy, if a bit more raw, collegiate performer to consider. Harris' athleticism certainly plays on the field. He has plus-speed that allows him to steal a ton of bases and cover a lot of ground in the outfield, where he has the chance to be an above-average defender with an average arm. His ability with the bat is what remains relatively unrefined. He is super short to the ball, more jabs than anything else, and he has a tendency to chase pitches, which leads to an elevated strikeout rate. There is a lot to work with, though, and it should be noted that because he switched focus to football rather than find a league to play baseball in over his summers, he's had relatively few at-bats. The upside is considerable, something a team is likely to bet on at some point during Day 2 of the Draft.

Though Kinneman had more success as a pitcher than as a hitter as a Pennsylvania high school senior, North Carolina State recruited him as an outfielder and installed him in its lineup early in his freshman season in 2015. While he provided solid production in his first two years with the Wolfpack, his struggles to make consistent contact with wood bats against top competition in the Cape Cod League depressed his Draft stock. It's on the rise this spring, however, as he has cut his strikeout rate and challenged for the NCAA Division I lead in home runs. Kinneman's surge stems from letting his bat speed and strength produce power naturally rather than worrying about home runs. While he has drawn walks throughout his college career, he's using a more disciplined approach and making hard contact on a more regular basis. He has above-average pop from the left side of the plate and a club that buys into his performance could take him in the second or third round. Kinneman's speed and arm elicit grades from fringy to solid. A team that believes he's on the high end of that range could try him in right field, though he has played in left at North Carolina State. He's a respectable athlete but most of his value will come from his bat.

The Notre Dame second baseman hit .312 with a .433 on-base percentage and .525 slugging percentage this year -- leading the Fighting Irish in all three slash line categories -- while adding eight home runs and nine stolen bases in 11 tries. Podkul doesn't wow you with his tools, but he's hit in the ACC, he has a good feel for the game, and he's a solid defender.

Donovan is a grinder who's done nothing but hit since high school, and this season he hit .302 with five homers and a team-high 24 extra-base hits for South Alabama. He has plus raw power from the left side, and although he has slightly below-average speed, Donovan is a decent third baseman with a solid arm who's been able to fill in at shortstop.

Quezada began his post-high school career at Cypress Junior College, pitching there for two seasons before moving on to Cal State Fullerton. He began the year pitching on Saturdays behind Colton Eastman, but after struggling initially moved to Sundays, where he righted the ship and upped his Draft stock late in the spring. Quezada isn't the biggest guy in the world at 6-foot-1, but he has a fearless approach on the mound, going right after hitters with his three-pitch mix. His fastball sits comfortably, and consistently, in the 90-94 mph range. His changeup might be his best secondary pitch, though he also has a slider that should be a Major League average breaking ball in time. Quezada throws a ton of strikes with a free and easy delivery, and is at his best when he is commanding his fastball well. The right-hander has the stuff and command of it to potentially be a back-end starter at the big league level. He did look good out of the bullpen while pitching in the Cape Cod League last summer, where his aggressive approach could fit well.

A three-year starter at Liberty, Artis has probably had his worst season, statistically, in this, his junior season. But the speedy outfielder has shown enough where teams that covet college performers will still have interest in the top five rounds It's not that Artis, a two-time Cape Cod League player, has been bad this spring; it's just that he set the bar high by winning the Big South Conference's Freshman of the Year in 2016 and then Player of the Year in 2017. He's proven he can really hit, albeit from an unorthodox crouch from the left side of the plate. There is some raw power, but he doesn't tap into it consistently, focusing on line-drive contact and drawing a lot more walks than strikeouts. He's a plus runner who can handle center field with below-average arm strength, one who could one day hit near the top of a big league lineup. Even in his "down year," Artis has gotten on base at an impressive clip and stolen bases. That skill set, not to mention his track record, has kept him fairly high on Draft boards.

Morris had Tommy John surgery in high school, but his fastball hits 97 mph and he has a very good changeup. The 6-foot-5, 222-pound right-hander doesn’t have a breaking ball, however, and his Draft stock will be based on the fastball velocity and ability to keep hitters off balance.

Schwarz seemed like a slam-dunk first-round pick for the 2017 Draft after his freshman season in 2015, but that dunk has somewhat gone off the back of the rim since with up-and-down sophomore and junior seasons. His bat has bounced back in his return for his senior campaign, but there are still questions about his future defensive home. Schwarz's best tool remains his tremendous raw power to all fields and his home run output kicked back up in 2018, and while he does draw walks, there is a good amount of swing and miss to his game. The son of one-time big league pitcher Jeff Schwarz does have some tools to catch, but his arm and receiving are fringe-average. A team looking to draft him in June will be buying the bat, with the hopes things can work out behind the plate enough for him to be an offensive-minded backstop, or in the belief he'll hit enough to play first base at the next level. While he is a senior, this may not be the kind of senior discount that has become de rigeur on Day 2 of the Draft.

Spears is an intimidating presence on the mound, with a 6-foot-7, 237-pound frame. The left-hander has a fastball that sits in the 89-93 mph range, along with an average changeup with a natural cut to it, and a decent breaking ball that he can command in the zone.

Murray’s fastball typically runs from 87-88 mph and can touch 91, and the right-hander features a good curveball and decent slider and changeup. All of those pitches are made much better by a deceptive delivery that confounds opposing hitters, particularly the fastball, which seems like it’s coming in much hotter than the upper-80s.

Culbertson has had forearm issues, but he has some good stuff, which starts with a big fastball that he throws 96 mph with very good run and sink and according to scouting reports can reach 98-to-100. The 6-foot-1, 220-pound right-hander also flashes an above-average hard slider and a decent changeup.

Tyler Jay set an Illinois record with 14 saves in 2015, when the Twins made him the No. 6 overall choice in the Draft. This spring, Gerber matched Jay by the end of the regular season and also climbed to within two of his Illini career mark of 24 saves. He won't go as high in the Draft, but he should be one of the first college relievers selected and fit in the first four or five rounds. A pure reliever who has never started a game in three years of college or during summer ball, Gerber attacks hitters with two pitches. He has sat at 92-96 mph with his fastball throughout the spring, even when working on consecutive days. His sharp 82-85 mph slider can be a plus pitch at times and is especially tough on right-handers. Gerber has a lot of flying parts in his funky delivery, which provides deception but also hampers his command at times. How well he can locate his pitches ultimately will determine if he reaches his ceiling as a set-up man. His strong 6-foot-4 frame and resilient arm make him a durable bullpen option and he should be one of the first 2018 Draftees to reach the big leagues.

Biggers is a ballplayer. He's performed strongly against tough competition in the SEC. Biggers is mainly a gap hitter, although the 5-foot-11, 180-pound lefty has some power to the pull side. He has average speed and a fringe arm in the field, but he has a quick release. A shortstop at Arkansas, Biggers might be more of a utility player in the future.

Kelly was emerging as a top high school prospect in West Virginia when he made the move to IMG Academy in Florida after his sophomore year. He's taken advantage of the opportunity, showing off premium arm strength against a very high level of competition. Kelly is all about arm strength and a big fastball. He'll sit 92-93 mph consistently and has been up to 95 mph at times. His slider, which has 10-to-4 action with solid bite and depth, gives him a breaking ball that will be at least average. It can get slurvy at times but he has made strides with it this spring. He doesn't use a changeup in games, though some have seen feel for a fringy one in bullpen sessions. The big right-hander has struggled with his command at times, with some feeling his high-tempo delivery and mindset point to a future in the bullpen. If a team thinks he's a starter, he could go in the top five rounds of the Draft because of his arm strength. Signing him away from his commitment to LSU might be another variable teams will consider in June.

Cropley has been strong both behind the plate and at the plate for Iowa, a good sign for the 5-foot-11, 185-pound senior catcher entering Draft day. He's an athletic, tough catcher who's a very good pitch receiver and blocker on balls in the dirt. He's got enough bat to carry him a hitter, with some pull power but a long swing. That didn't stop his production for the Hawkeyes: Cropley led the team in batting average (.342), on-base percentage (.449) and slugging percentage (.578) this season, with nine homers, 50 RBIs and 30 walks to only 28 strikeouts.

Hansen’s fastball reaches 96 mph, but the velocity tends to fade fast as he gets deeper into a start. But the right-hander has a good changeup and an improving hybrid pitch -- a slurve -- with a quick arm.

While the high school arms in other parts of the country get more attention, Pipkin made sure Northern California would be visited frequently by scouts when he kept pace with top prep prospect Ethan Hankins (Georgia) velocity-wise at the Area Code Games over the summer. His stock took a hit when he broke out of the gate poorly this spring, but he was righting the ship as his senior season wore on. Tall, athletic and projectable physically, Pipkin screams upside potential. With a quick arm and long frame, he was up to 95-96 mph at Area Codes and maintained his velocity across two innings of work, though he was more in the 92-93 mph range early this spring. He's demonstrated the makings of good secondary offerings as well, flashing a plus slider and showing feel for a changeup, giving him the chance to have three at least average pitches at his disposal. All of it comes from an easy and clean delivery. The biggest question mark around Pipkin is with his command and control, which can be shaky at times. There's reason to hope that his athleticism and delivery will lend itself to harnessing his stuff, especially as he matures. His ceiling could be entice a team to draft him early enough to not head down the road to California for college ball.

Montes de Oca remains one of the biggest enigmas in the Draft, perhaps the biggest. He had Tommy John surgery as a Missouri high school junior in 2013 and ulnar nerve transposition three years later, and he turned down the White Sox as a 14th-rounder in 2014 and the Nationals as a 15th-rounder last summer. While all that is off-putting to a lot of teams, when he's on he might have the most devastating pair of pitches available in this year's crop. Montes de Oca can work at 93-98 mph and reach 100 with exceptionally heavy sink on his fastball, which can get top-of-the-scale grades for both its velocity and its life. His breaking ball has gone from a power slurve to more of a true slider that climbs into the upper 80s and is a wipeout offering at its best. Both pitches were overpowering when he worked the first seven innings of a no-hitter against Maryland-Baltimore County in March, when he struck out 12 of the 23 batters he faced and permitted just two balls to be hit in the air. After continuing to excel in the rotation over the next month, Montes de Oca pitched just 11 innings over the final seven weeks of the regular season without any explanation as to why his role changed. His lack of a reliable changeup and his inability to throw consistent strikes point to a future in the bullpen. With his stuff, he could move rapidly as a reliever and have closer upside if he stays healthy.

Cohen showed the quality of his stuff in his April 13 outing against Southern Alabama and Travis Swaggerty, the No. 11 prospect in this year's Draft. Cohen has a lot of weapons. The 6-foot-1 right-hander was 92-to-98 mph with his fastball over five innings, along with a power curveball and changeup. Cohen struck out seven in the game, and although Swaggerty had a single off him, he also got Swaggerty to fly out and pop up.

Georgia is a College World Series national seed this year in large part thanks to McGovern, who was named First-Team All-SEC and a Baseball America Third Team All-American after hitting .324 with a team-high 16 homers. The 6-foot-2 outfielder can really hit, and he's showing his power at the plate. He's a below-average runner and defender, but he's lost 25 pounds.

Lee has battled inconsistency on the mound and saw time in multiple roles this season -- at one point, he was the Friday night starter for the Ragin' Cajuns, but he was later moved into the closer role -- after dealing with injury in 2017. But he's only two years removed from being the 2016 Sun Belt Freshman of the Year and the recipient of several Freshman All-America honors. Lee sits 91-to-93 mph and can reach 95 with his fastball, and his changeup is very good when it's on. His curveball is below average though, with no bite, and he has command issues.

Eichhorn was named First-Team All-Sun Belt this season after striking out 106 batters in 88 2/3 innings with a 3.15 ERA. The 6-foot-1, 220-pound junior throws 92-to-93 mph as a starter, with a slider that can be above-average at its best and an average changeup. He throws strikes and has good pitchability, and there's a chance he could be a No. 4 starter down the road.

Stokes has had a substantive injury history (foot in 2016, wrist in ‘17 and hamstring in ‘18) and not much success to this point his his collegiate career, but he does have raw power and good speed. He’s also versatile, able to play first base and potentially shortstop.

The 6-foot-3, 225-pound right-hander out of UNC Wilmington has a fastball that ranges from 88-92 mph and can touch 95. He also features a 12-6 power curveball with a deceptive delivery. The question for Cota will be whether he can hold his stuff consistently from one start to the next.

The Mississippi State commit saw his fastball velocity increase by 4-5 mph toward the end of the prep season, to the mid-90s. His breaking ball was also better in that span, but he has to overcome a delivery that isn’t mechanically sound.
Harris profiles as a corner outfielder with average tools but gap-to-gap power and good speed. He also has a smooth swing and good plate discipline, resulting in a high walk count.

Broom's biggest 2018 highlight came on April 24, when the sidewinding right-hander struck out 12 batters in six relief innings against No. 1 Florida to lead Mercer past the Gators. Broom was named a Third Team All-American by Baseball America after recording a 1.70 ERA and 111 strikeouts in 74 innings this season. The 6-foot-1 junior has great makeup. His fastball sits 89-to-92 mph, and he also features a slicing breaking ball and good changeup.

Higginbotham has screws inserted in his pitching elbow following a stress fracture, but pitches with maximum effort on every pitch. His fastball ranges from 90-92 mph, and can reach 96. The left-hander has an average breaking ball and changeup, and can be inconsistent.

McGowan was named Third Team All-Big Ten for his junior season, as well as making the All-Big Ten Tournament Team. The 6-foot-2, 212-pound first baseman led the Boilermakers to the College World Series Regionals, hitting .302/.442/.533 with a team-high 12 home runs and 58 RBIs. McGowan's main tool is his power: He can lift the ball and has done a better job at laying off offspeed pitches this year, although he has a long swing. McGowan isn't a good defensive first baseman.
The Texas A&M second baseman has a small frame but barrels the ball consistently and has decent speed. He was named the 2017 NJCAA Player of the Year while with Hutchinson (KS).

Thompson has an electric fastball. He sits in the mid-90s even late into games and can push his heater up to 98 -- or even 100. His breaking ball can be an excellent pitch, too. But he hasn't had the stats to match the stuff this season, with a 6.82 ERA in 33 innings for Louisville, and 35 strikeouts. As far as health, Thompson had Tommy John surgery in 2016, and he's also missed time with a shoulder issue. He's been drafted twice before -- by the Reds out of high school in the 37th round and by the Yankees last year in the 27th round, after being ranked No. 164 in MLB Pipeline's Top 200 Draft prospects.

Alexander, whose father played in the Minor Leagues with the Cleveland Indians, was high on follow lists after a summer showcase circuit that included both the Perfect Game All-American Classic and Baseball Factory's UnderArmour All-America Game. An uneven spring has tempered enthusiasm somewhat, though there are still tools to be excited about. Alexander's carrying tool is his arm, which grades out as an 80 in terms of pure strength, though he'll need to work on accuracy with his throws across the diamond. He has good enough hands and range to stay at shortstop long-term. There are more questions about his bat. He does have some raw power and some bat speed, but there has been enough swing-and-miss to worry some scouts about how his bat will translate at the next level. A team taking the South Carolina commit in the top four or five rounds will believe the hit tool will come with pro instruction. He won't get drafted because of this, but knowing he is 93-95 mph off the mound, albeit as a raw thrower, is a fallback.

One of the better two-way talents in the 2018 Draft class, Northcut has a fastball that peaks in the low 90s and a promising breaking ball. He shows even more promise as a hitter, enough to get selected as early as the third round. Whether that will be high enough to lure him away from a Vanderbilt commitment remains to be seen. Northcut's quick right-handed swing with loft and his strength combine to generate impressive raw power, which he showed off by finishing second in the home run derby at the Perfect Game All-American Classic last August. He lets his pop come naturally and uses the entire field, showing the potential to hit for average as well. He has a track record of performing well against top competition on the showcase circuit, starring on the loaded Canes Baseball travel team. While Northcut is a well below-average runner, he moves well enough to make plays at third base. His hands and arm strength are assets, and he could become a solid defender if he can improve his agility. He'll have to maintain the conditioning of his thick frame to remain at the hot corner.

Kolek is a strike-thrower with a good mix of pitches in his arsenal. The right-hander projects as a No. 5 starter at the professional level, with a 90-91 mph fastball that can touch 93, a good slider and a good changeup.

Carranza spent the first two years of his college career at Oregon, but transferred to Division II Asuza Pacific for his junior season. After a strong showing in the Northwoods League over the summer, he has stepped in as the Cougars' ace and has helped them be one of the top DII teams in the country. Now 6-foot-5, Carranza has some projection, with the chance to add velocity as he adds strength to his frame. He's already up to 95 mph with his fastball and comfortably sits around 92 mph. His low-80s slider is inconsistent, but shows depth and tilt enough to make you think it can be an average pitch in the future. He'll flash a solid changeup with fade on occasion as well. Carranza has missed bats this year at a good clip, but command has eluded him, both in and out of the strike zone, leading to a high walk rate and getting hit more than he should. Still, his live arm and good frame could point to a future in the back of a rotation, enough for a team to consider him in the top six rounds.

Outfielder Mike Siani is the highest-rated player in the state of Pennsylvania, and for good reason. But in many ways Guilbe, a product of Berks Catholic High School in Reading, has outperformed his fellow Keystone State prepster this spring. Guilbe definitely had some helium as the spring finally warmed up and the Draft approached, putting up a season that put him in contention for the state's Gatorade Player of the Year award. He has legitimate right-handed power coming from a strong, athetic body. He's more strength over bat speed with a bit of an uphill swing, something he'll likely need to shorten to ensure he taps into that raw pop consistently at the next level. A below-average runner, there are some questions about his ability to stay at the hot corner because of his hands and lack of range. He is fairly physically mature. There were several teams looking at Guilbe in the top five rounds. Those are the teams that believe his bat will translate and he'll hit enough to make his power a plus tool and make it worthwhile to lure him away from his University of Tennessee commitment.

Kingham, brother of Pirates right-hander Nick Kingham, once featured a fastball that ran from 94 to 97 mph with life, but that’s been down to the 88-91 mph range -- sometimes reaching 93 -- of late. He also has an average slider that used to run from 86-89 mph. He’s pitched well of late, helping Texas reach the NCAA Super Regional round by pitching into the eighth inning against Texas A&M during Sunday’s 8-3 victory.

An imposing presence on the mound, Leban stands at 6-foot-3 at 245 pounds. This season, the big right-hander made 26 relief appearances and recorded 12 saves as the Jayhawks' closer, while collecting 36 strikeouts. Leban has a big fastball that gets up to 96 mph, and he flashes a slurve in the 80-to-83 mph range that grades out as average. Leban has a better fastball and command than teammate and fellow Draft prospect Codi Heuer, but Leban's breaking ball isn't as good.

Howell was drafted by the Royals in the 19th round in 2017 out of junior college, but he elected to stay another season. He hit .401 in 60 games for his Kirkwood team, and he stole 40 bases. Howell is extremely athletic, with 70-to-80-grade speed, but he's very raw, and teams will have to do a lot of projecting on his bat. A 6-foot-2, 180-pound shortstop for now, Howell could be a center fielder in the future. He could also be headed to Iowa next year, depending on the results of the Draft.

An all-state quarterback as a Pennsylvania high schooler, Datres has been a three-year starter for North Carolina at third base and has steadily improved his numbers each season. Draft-eligible as a 21-year-old sophomore in 2017, he went unpicked because of signability concerns. This year, he's arguably the best college third baseman available after lock first-rounders Alec Bohm (Wichita State) and Jonathan India (Florida) and could land in the first five rounds. Datres has gotten in better shape this season, adding strength and bat speed. He shows solid raw power in batting practice but it hasn't translated that way in game action, where most of his pop comes in the form of doubles. He makes consistent contact and draws a decent amount of walks, but he may need to get more aggressive and hit more home runs to fit the offensive profile at third base. Datres has average speed and moves well on the bases and at the hot corner. His arm and defensive ability earn average to solid grades from scouts, and some wonder if he might be able to handle second base. He may be more of a utilityman than an everyday third baseman if he doesn't do more at the plate.

Lewis racked up the strikeouts for Kentucky this season, with 92 in his 72 2/3 innings over 12 starts. He's an athletic pitcher who stands tall at 6-foot-7, and he's very consistent on the mound. Lewis' fastball sits around 90 mph, and he has a very good changeup -- he just tends to overuse it. The right-hander doesn't have a great breaking ball. Lewis has been drafted twice: He was a 19th-round pick by the Padres out of high school in 2014, and an 11th-round pick by the Rays last year.

One of the best high school pitching prospects ever to come out of Delaware, Bilous missed his entire senior season following Tommy John surgery but still got offered $600,000 by the Dodgers after they took him in the 29th round of the 2015 Draft. He contributed to Coastal Carolina's 2016 College World Series championship as a freshman and unquestionably has a first-round arm. Yet he has battled the strike zone throughout three years with the Chanticleers and two summers in the Cape Cod League, making it unclear how high he'll get picked this June. Bilous has one of the best pure fastballs among college starters, sitting at 92-95 mph and topping out at 97 with sink. In shorter stints on the Cape last summer, he reached 99. His slider combines mid-80s power and late bite, serving as a wipeout pitch at its best, and he can flash a plus changeup with fade as well. Bilous is athletic but has a long arm action in the back of his delivery that hampers him from repeating his release point and keeping his mechanics in sync. He has averaged at least seven walks per nine innings in each of his three college seasons and may never have more than fringy control. He's probably best suited for the bullpen and has the stuff to handle a high-leverage role if he can find the plate.

Kone has the tools, but needs some refinement at shortstop and at the plate. He’s got average speed and arm strength, and needs some work on his approach around the bag at second base, as well as offensively.

Heasley is fresh off a complete game in the NCAA Deland Regional, helping Oklahoma State avoid elimination by surrendering just one unearned run while striking out seven against South Florida on Sunday. The tall right-hander could be a top-three-round talent, but also can be inconsistent from start to start. His fastball ranges from 92-94 mph, and he has curveball with good depth. He also throws a slider and changeup for which he changes his arm slot.

Schmid features a sinking fastball that runs from 86-90 mph, as well as a good curveball and a very good changeup. The 6-foot-1, 195-pound left-hander is a strike-thrower and has shown to be a competitor when faced with adversity.

Martin made an immediate impact at Texas Tech in 2015, leading the Big 12 Conference and all NCAA Division I freshmen with 10 wins, including the Red Raiders' first-ever victory at the College World Series. He enjoyed similar success as a sophomore before missing nearly two months with shoulder tendinitis, then got off to a brilliant start this year only to fade in late April. Scouts aren't sure exactly what to make of a guy they projected as a second- or third-rounder coming into 2018. While Martin doesn't have a true plus pitch, he knows how to mix two versions of a fastball and two effective secondary offerings. He'll usually work with a sinking two-seam fastball at 89-92 mph, though he can reach back for a mid-90s four-seamer a couple of times per game. His tight slider is usually his best secondary pitch, though some scouts prefer his changeup, which he'll throw in any count. Martin has a strong frame that lends itself more to durability than projection. He usually pounds the strike zone, though his control and command deserted him toward the end of the regular season. Scouts love his mound presence and guile, and he'll catch unsuspecting hitters off guard by quick-pitching them.

Varland was a machine for Concordia this season, going 7-1 with a 1.04 ERA and 79 strikeouts in 60 1/3 innings. He threw 96 mph in his first game in March, and he's kept his velocity all year, while coupling his fastball with a solid slider. The 6-foot-1, 190-pound right-hander is physically mature and has a good makeup. He projects as a relief pitcher down the line.

Although he had a down year in 2018 -- hitting .231/.346/.389 with nine home runs in 56 games -- Aiello has a power bat and strong arm from third base. In 2017, he slashed .328/.417/.643 with 20 homers in 63 games. He also played for the United States Collegiate National Team in 2017.

TNXL Baseball Academy was started a few years ago in Florida as a place for high-level high school level players to focus more of their day on baseball, while taking care of classwork online through the Florida Virtual School. Cabell has been one of the better products of the academy to date, with some serious upside, though it does not come without risk. There is no question that Cabell looks the part of an impactful offensive player and has the bat skills to go along with it. He has tremendous raw power with the ability to drive the ball with the kind of different sound you hear from some hitters when he makes contact. With that power, however, comes some considerable swing and miss issues, as scouts see a grooved swing. It goes a long way when the ball is thrown in that groove, but he doesn't always adjust when it's not. A fringy rurnner, Cabell is a capable outfielder with a very strong arm, and he gets high marks for his makeup. If a team believes he can make enough contact to tap into that power, it could see him as a future run-producing right fielder and take him early enough to keep him from heading to LSU.
Fletcher features an 88-92 mph fastball following Tommy John surgery, and has a deceptive delivery. He also throws a curveball that’s a tick better than average.

Holland impressed on the showcase circuit last summer and has established himself as the best up-the-middle position prospect among Texas high schoolers this spring. He could fit in the first four or five rounds, though whether that will be early enough to sign him away from a commitment to Texas remains to be seen. As the son of noted Houston-area hitting coach Sid Holland, it's no surprise that Holland has a quality right-handed swing. He doesn't try to do too much at the plate, satisfying himself with hitting line drives from gap to gap. He has some bat speed but isn't very physical, so most of his power will come in the form of doubles. Holland's best tool is his plus speed, though he's still learning how to get the most out of it as a basestealer and defender. He's a solid center fielder with average arm strength. While he can play all three outfield positions, he doesn't have the pop to profile on the corners.

Frisbee’s 94 mph fastball could be a 97 mph heater if the 6-foot-5 right-hander comes out of the bullpen in the professional ranks. The 2018 Southern Conference Pitcher of the Year posted a 3.45 ERA with 116 strikeouts in 91 1/3 innings for UNC Greensboro this season.

Williams probably projects as a corner outfielder at the next level, but he's also a left-handed pitcher who throws in the upper 80s. As a hitter, he has a nice loose swing from the left side and some power. In the outfield, the Florida International commit has shown an above-average arm, according to scouting reports.
The sophomore right-hander throws his fastball 89-92 mph, up to 95. He’s got average secondary pitches, but four pitches that he can throw for strikes. Willrodt is a Texas State commit.

The 6-foot-3 righty was a workhorse for Central Michigan, leading the team with 15 starts and 93 innings pitched this season, including two complete games. He's flashed very good stuff at times. Brettell has a 90-to-94 mph fastball with darting sink and maintains his velocity, but he gets hit if he leaves the ball up. Brettell also has a slider to work with his fastball, although his changeup floats a little too much.

Bordner racked up 10 saves and 28 strikeouts in 18 2/3 innings as Louisville's closer early this season, but he hasn't pitched since April 17 because of elbow inflammation. As a sophomore and in the Cape Cod League last summer, Bordner had a fastball in the 87-to-93 mph range and touched 95. But this year, it's dropped to around 87-to-90, and the pitch isn't as deceptive. The 6-foot-6, 240-pound right-hander also has some work to do on his secondary stuff once healthy. Bordner's slider grades out as slightly below-average, and his changeup as average.

Teel is a crafty left-hander with average raw stuff that he is able to compete with by changing arm slots and using good command. He features a fastball that could regularly hit 94 mph if out of the bullpen, as well as a changeup and developing slider.

An Arizona high school product who originally committed to North Carolina, Dillard wound up at San Jacinto (Texas) JC instead, making him Draft-eligible as a freshman this spring. He and McLennan (Texas) CC catcher/right-hander Josh Breaux excited scouts when they both hit 98 mph while facing each other on Feb. 9, but both have pitched sparingly since while dealing with elbow issues. Dillard has added significant physical strength in the last year, boosting his fastball from 88-92 mph as a prep starter to 93-98 mph as a JUCO reliever. His breaking ball has gotten quicker as well, becoming a power slurve with curveball break and slider velocity at 82-86 mph. As impressive as his pure stuff is, he requires a lot of polish. Dillard has a faster-paced tempo and some effort in his delivery that adds deception but also leads to unreliable command and control. If he can stay healthy and do a better job of locating his pitches, he could become a high-leverage reliever. He has committed to Missouri for 2019 if he doesn't turn pro this summer.

Olenek profiles as a second baseman at the professional level, though he also plays in the outfield. His bat hasn’t been all that productive to this point, and there hasn’t been much pop. Though he’s 6-foot-5, he’s got good speed and a strong arm.

Last season, Hulsizer was the NCAA Division I home run champion, crushing 27 long balls in 2017 and winning the College Home Run Derby. This year, the Morehead State outfielder missed 21 games with a fractured hamate bone in his left hand -- but in the 42 games he has played, he's hit .320/.428/.621 with 12 more homers. Hulsizer has 70-grade raw power. He's a big body at 6-foot-2 and 225 pounds, he's strong, and he has a huge swing from the right side. Hulsizer is an above-average runner, too, with an average arm, but his contact hitting and defense aren't as good.

school.name: University of Science and Arts of Oklahoma

picks.blurb: Wolfe has very good speed, but needs refinement at shortstop. A 12th-round selection of the Pirates in 2017, the Texas Christian commit has a rigid swing and little pop, though he shows potential at the plate.

The 6-foot-5, 225-pound right-hander has a fastball with run that tops out around 97 mph, and he struck out 14 in his final start of the season for Tennessee against Missouri. Linginfelter also features an average slurve that runs in the low-to-mid-80s.

The LSU outfielder is a very good runner and contact hitter -- a line drive and gap hitter more so than power. Duplantis has helped spark the Tigers all year on their way to the College World Series Regionals, hitting .336 with 18 stolen bases. Defensively, he has the ability to play center field even without a great arm. But he's played right field at LSU, which has No. 65 Draft prospect Zach Watson in center.

school.name: State College of Florida Manatee - Sarasota

picks.blurb: Though he’s been a starting pitcher at Texas Tech, Kilian’s fastball would play better as a reliever, as it could hit 95 mph regularly. The sophomore right-hander has an average changeup and a good, albeit inconsistent breaking ball.

Montverde High School in Florida is best known for producing Indians All-Star Francisco Lindor and the school's senior class this year is headlined by shortstop Nander De Sedas. With a strong spring, Wright has been grabbing some of that spotlight as he aims to become the first pitcher drafted from the school. There's a lot to like in this 6-foot-5 right-hander's stuff, starting with his pure arm strength. Wright has been up to 96 mph at times this spring, though he usually sits in the 91-92 mph range and hasn't always held his velocity deep into his starts. He doesn't always use his lower half effectively and doing so should allow him to maintain his velo. He throws an average slider that has three-quarter tilt to it when it's on, but often looks more like a hard slurve. Like many high school pitchers, his changeup is developing. Size and arm strength can't be taught and both are boxes scouts check off when it comes to Wright. Some mechanical adjustments could help him take off at the next level, with perhaps enough to work with to sign him away from his Louisville commitment.

New England isn't typically a hotbed for high school pitching talent, though a good arm comes from the region from time to time. In this year's Draft class, right-hander Mike Vasil is the top talent, even with his injury, and while Hajjar isn't in the same conversation, he is the best left-hander from the area. Aside from his left-handedness, Hajjar has size, stuff and projectability working in his favor. The 6-foot-4 southpaw has room to grow into his frame, which means there could me more to his fastball in the future, which he can throw anywhere from 86 to 93 mph, sitting comfortably right around 89-90 mph. His slider projects as at least average, if not more, once he tightens it more consistently. It can have some slurve action to it, but it has enough depth to be a potential swing and miss pitch. He doesn't use a changeup at all and hasn't needed it at his high school level. He has a funky delivery, all arms and legs, which adds deception, but he still throws strikes. Scouts have watched Hajjar carefully, particularly since last summer's Area Code Games, and were running in to see him as the weather warmed in New England. He's committed to the University of Michigan, but a team might be willing to gamble on his upside from the left side to take him early enough to sign.

After a strong summer in the California Collegiate League, Campbell seemed poised for a breakout in 2017. Instead, he made just one appearance before getting shut down with elbow soreness that necessitated the removal of several bone spurs. He has better pure stuff than Arkansas rotationmate Blaine Knight, a potential second-rounder, but he hasn't had the same success, and questions about his health and signability as a redshirt sophomore cloud his Draft status. When he's 100 percent, Campbell's fastball usually ranges from 91-95 mph and can reach 98. His heater is fairly straight, but can be tough to lift because his high three-quarters delivery creates steep downhill plane. Both his curveball and slider/cutter show flashes of becoming plus pitches, but they lack consistency and get very hittable when they flatten out. Campbell also has a decent changeup with fade, giving him four usable offerings, but he'll have to improve his control, command and durability to remain a starter. He doesn't always stay on top of his pitches, and he missed a start with elbow inflammation this spring while struggling to maintain the quality of his stuff past the first few innings. If he reaches the big leagues, he'll be the first Portugal-born player to do so since 1875.

The 6-foot-3, 208-pound left-hander’s fastball sat in the 92-95 mph range before he underwent Tommy John surgery in 2016, and the velocity hasn’t returned to that level. It now sits between 89 and 90 mph in his first season back. But Small is a strike-thrower and features a deceptive delivery.

Schanuel was drafted twice before ending up at Iowa -- in the 36th round by the A's out of high school in 2015 and in the 20th round by the Phillies out of junior college in '16. The 6-foot-3, 180-pound right-hander has pitched as both a starter and a reliever. He's been better in relief, when his fastball plays up to 93-to-96 mph, a level he hasn't matched as a starting pitcher. Schanuel also flashes an average slider and changeup but has room to work on his delivery.

Back in 2011, a young shortstop came out of Montverde High School in Florida and ended up going No. 8 overall to the Cleveland Indians. While he had a long way to go before earning a true comp to All-Star Francisco Lindor, De Sedas' strong summer had him brought up in the conversation for more reasons than just attending the same high school, though an uneven spring has cooled his projections considerably. The raw tools are still there for De Sedas to be a successful middle infielder at the next level, but he hasn't put them to consistent use in his senior year. He has plus raw power and has shown the wherewithal to tap into it in games at times in the past. He hasn't shown the same advanced approach he had over the summer, but the skills are there to hit for average and tap into that raw power. Relatively new to switch-hitting, his left-handed swing needs work. De Sedas has a very strong arm and good hands, which gives him the chance to stick at short. His lack of speed could eventually necessitate a move to third. De Sedas' summer moved him way up on Draft lists and raised the level of expectations considerably, but he didn't come out and perform as hoped this spring. A team that feels it can help him unlock those raw tools consistently will still take him early enough to keep him from heading to Florida State.

Walton High (Marietta, Ga.) is a baseball powerhouse, winning three state titles, sending seven players to the big leagues and getting six players drafted in the top three rounds. Bartnicki entered 2018 as a good bet to increase that last number to seven, but he hasn't thrown as well as he did on the showcase circuit last summer. While he still offers more upside than the typical prep left-hander, clubs don't think they can take him high enough to sign him away from his Georgia Tech commitment. After sitting at 92-93 mph and touching 95 at times with angle and run on his fastball last summer, Bartnicki has seen his velocity drop 2-3 mph for much of this spring. His slider has similarly regressed, losing some power and bite and rarely looking like the potential plus pitch he flashed in the past. His changeup wasn't quite as deceptive as it had been previously, though he has the makings of an average third offering. Bartnicki has a strong frame and the athleticism to throw strikes, though his control also has wavered this year. His delivery is clean but also somewhat slow and gives batters a good look at his pitches. He has a ceiling as a mid-rotation starter but will need plenty of patience and development to get there.

South Carolina hasn't had a high school left-hander taken in the first five rounds since the Phillies popped Mike Martin at No. 5 overall in 1970. McDaniels, a two-way star on Pee Dee Academy's (Mullins) 2-A state championship team in 2017, is talented enough to break that drought. He also could blossom into a high pick three years from now if he attends Coastal Carolina. While McDaniels is more about projection than present stuff, he's a southpaw with life on his fastball and feel for spin. His fastball usually ranges from 86-92 mph with sink and should develop into a solid offering as he adds strength to his slender frame. His curveball already shows flashes of becoming a plus pitch with some power and depth. McDaniels also has some aptitude for throwing a changeup. He's athletic and repeats his delivery well, working down in the zone from a three-quarters arm slot.

There is no question that Noah Naylor is the top prospect from Canada in this year's Draft class, separating himself from the pack. Cerantola is No. 2, and the top pitching prospect north of the border, with interested teams looking at his upside potential more than what he brings to the table now. Cerantola's now stuff isn't bad, it's just raw. He was up to 93 mph with his fastball this spring and presently sits in the 90-91 mph range. With a 6-foot-6 frame that should take on good weight and strength, it's easy to dream on more velocity in the future. His secondary stuff needs work, but he'll show a solid mid-70s curveball at times with good depth at times and has shown some feel for his changeup. He does use his size well and throws downhill. A solid athlete, he repeats his delivery which should help him improve his control. Canada has produced some very good pitching talent over the years, most recently in the first round with Mike Soroka in 2015 and last year with second-rounder Landon Leach. Cerantola isn't quite in that neck of the woods, but there's enough ceiling to perhaps entice a team to sign him away from his commitment to Mississippi State.

Minnesota's top pitching prospect often has the two-way talent to pull double duty at the college level, as was the case with Logan Shore (2012), who blossomed into an Athletics second-rounder after three years at Florida, and Sam Carlson (2017), who signed with the Mariners for $2 million as a second-rounder last June. It's true again this spring with Halvorsen, which may make him follow through on a commitment to Missouri. He wants to play shortstop and pro teams clearly prefer him on the mound. Halvorsen throws harder than most high schoolers, hitting 96 mph while earning a save in the Perfect Game All-American Classic last August and pumping the second-quickest fastball (95.4 mph) at the World Wood Bat Assocation World Championship last October. Though horrible weather this spring delayed his senior season until mid-April, he regularly threw 92-95 once he took the mound. He generates his velocity with arm speed rather than effort and has some armside run on his heater. Halvorsen has decent feel for his curveball and changeup but both are works in progress because he has spent more time on his development as a position player. He threw a slider last summer, which some scouts preferred to his curve. He has the raw power, arm strength and athleticism to profile as a college third baseman but won't get the opportunity to hit if he does turn pro

Engelmann is a plus runner -- the speedy Michigan outfielder stole 21 bases this season. He can swing the bat, too, hitting .351 for the Wolverines, although he doesn't have a ton of pop. But he's made some key improvements at the plate, with a smoother swing, improved lift and better pitch selection.

The Southeastern Conference freshman of the year and batting champion (.408) in 2016, Mangum hasn't quite matched that production since but has continued to catalyze Mississippi State's lineup the last two years. Draft-eligible as a 21-year-old sophomore in 2017, he turned down the Yankees as a 30th-round pick, lasting that long only because of questionable signability. He may have the best football family in this year's Draft, as his grandfather John Sr., father John Jr. and uncle Kris all played professionally. Mangum's best tool is his speed, which grades as plus-plus out of the batter's box but doesn't play quite that fast and hasn't translated into elite basestealing or defensive ability. A switch-hitter, he concentrates on making contact and getting on base, an approach that leaves him with almost no power. He's doing a better job of working counts and drawing walks as a junior, though he still needs more patience to become a true tablesetter. Mangum also has improved his jumps and reads this year, making him a solid defender in center field. He has a stronger arm than most players at his position, having reached 93 mph with his fastball when he started five games on the mound as a sophomore. He proved his grit by playing through a broken left hand for much of last season.

Sharts' personal pitching coach is his uncle Scott, who starred at Simi Valley High School in the late 1980s and hit 32 homers in three seasons. While his nephew does hit, and could be a two-way player at the University of Nevada, he's really stood out on the mound at his uncle's alma mater. Sharts has a three-pitch mix and isn't afraid to throw any of them. He's been largely in the 90-92 mph range now, though some have seen him up to 94 mph, jumping from 85-88 mph as a junior thanks to an extensive offseason workout regimen. His 72-75 mph curve could be a Major League average pitch in the future, but it's his changeup that's his better secondary pitch, an 82-84 mph offspeed pitch that has the chance to be plus. The best player to come out of Simi Valley High School in terms of big league impact has been a pitcher, Scott Radinsky. Sharts doesn't project to go as early as Radinsky's second-round selection in 1986, but he could go in the top five rounds, becoming the first in his high school to get taken since 2009.

After starring on a pitching staff that included five future high school first-rounders and won a gold medal at the 18-and-under World Cup in Japan in September 2015, Bergner had first-round aspirations of his own. But an inconsistent prep senior season and worries about his mechanics rendered him unsignable, so he headed to North Carolina after turning down the Red Sox as a 38th-rounder in the 2016 Draft. Now a similar scenario is unfolding this year, when Berger has followed a banner summer in the Cape Cod League with an up-and-down spring and may not get picked high enough as a Draft-eligible sophomore to turn pro. Bergner can show flashes of three plus pitches but doesn't produce that kind of stuff on a regular basis. He often worked with a mid-90s fastball on the Cape, but he sat in the low 90s this spring, and his heater is hittable because it lacks much life. His best secondary offering is a mid-80s changeup with fade, though he lacks feel to spin and doesn't trust his curveball. The biggest issue with Bergner is his delivery, which is long in back and short in front. It affects the quality and command of his pitches and also allows hitters to get a good look at them. The hope is that he can clean things up and maybe become a mid-rotation starter, but the consensus is that he'll be a reliever who will have to work heavily off his fastball.

A New Jersey high school right-hander hasn't been taken in the top five round since Joe Gatto was a second-rounder back in 2014, and Gatto was the first one since 2008 (Jason Knapp, second round). Ras, thanks to his arm strength and athleticism, has the chance to join a very select group. Ras is a two-way stand out at Middletown High School who has separated himself as a pitching prospect. He's projectable at 6-foot-4 and has been up to 93 mph with his fastball, with more in the tank as he fills out that frame. He's at his best when he stays on top and throws downhill, creating good life on that heater. His secondary stuff is behind his fastball, though his slurvy breaking ball and his changeup show glimpses of being solid offerings. Some scouts don't love his arm action and delivery, but with his athleticism, there's hope things could be corrected with instruction. There are teams that don't shy away from pitching projects, and Ras certainly would be one. He's committed to the University of Alabama, but it's easy to dream on what he can become once he's focusing only on the mound.

Hess' electric arm could have made him a top-five-rounds pick out of a Virginia high school in 2016, but his steadfast commitment to Louisiana State dropped him all the way to the Yankees in the 35th round. He opened his freshman season as a midweek starter before becoming a dominating closer in April and playing a key role in the Tigers' runner-up finish at the College World Series. He moved into the weekend rotation this spring to fill a need, and though most scouts still envision him as a reliever, he's talented enough to go in the first three rounds as a Draft-eligible sophomore. As a starter, Hess usually works with a 91-95 mph fastball and a solid slider. When he came out of the bullpen last year, he repeatedly ran his heater into upper 90s and backed it up with a wipeout slider. He uses his low-three-quarters arm slot and 6-foot-6 frame to create angle and life that make both pitches difficult to contend with. Hess has yet to show much aptitude for a changeup, but also hasn't really needed one yet. He has toned down his delivery some but still throws with effort, and his arm action and fringy control may have him ticketed for the bullpen in pro ball. If a pro team makes him a reliever, he could be one of the first players in the 2018 Draft to reach the big leagues.

Cypress Ranch High (Cypress, Texas) has a formidable pitching duo in Madden and fellow right-hander Matt Thompson, a junior who's a potential first-rounder for 2019. Madden could factor into the top three rounds this June after his fastball touched 97 mph during the state playoffs. But the Texas recruit is about more than just pure velocity. Madden usually pitches at 90-93 mph with his four-seam fastball and at 87-90 mph with sink and run on his two-seamer. If he adds some more strength and does a better job of using his legs in his delivery, he could reach the mid-90s more regularly. His hard low-80s slider and fading 78-82 changeup play off his heater well and give him two more potential plus offerings. Madden uses his size to create angle and repeats his fairly sound mechanics. He has good feel for pitching and all of the ingredients to become a No. 3 starter.

Poland will definitely hit -- he has a compact swing with quick hands and bat speed -- and the Louisville commit might develop some power, too. The 6-foot, 190-pound second baseman isn't as good defensively, though, with heavy legs and footwork in the field. Poland was a Rawlings-Perfect Game Third Team All-American for 2018.
Watson is likely a seventh-to-10th round selection. The outfielder has raw power from the right side, as well as a decent arm and defensive ability in a corner outfield spot.

Las Vegas has produced some serious hitters in recent years, guys like Kris Bryant and Bryce Harper. Bishop Gorman High School has contributed to that list as Joey Gallo's alma mater. While Wells may never have been a first-round candidate, a strong start to his summer certainly put the catcher more firmly on the map, but an elbow injury that has kept him from catching all spring has made his stock tougher to figure out. While Wells hasn't thrown competitively since last summer, scouts could still evaluate his bat this spring, and he does have some ability there. He can impact the baseball with a solid hit tool and a good amount of raw power to tap into. Frequently pitched around this spring, he's shown he can be patient and not try to do too much. When healthy and playing defensively, he has shown decent receiving skills and an accurate arm, though that obviously is a concern. Without plus tools, Wells' best selling point was his all-around package as a big strong left-handed hitter who can stay behind the plate. If he can show teams his arm is fine, it's the kind of skillset that is always in high demand.

Binelas has shown potential at the plate, but defensively, he lacks the arm and agility to play third base regularly at the next level, so first base or left field could be a future destination for him. A comp could be Travis Shaw.

The tall 6-foot-6 right-hander gets his fastball up to 95 mph, and he has a plus changeup to pair with it, as well as a slurvy breaking ball. Hoeing has had a successful season for Louisville, with a 2.88 ERA in 65 2/3 innings, but that success comes with the caveat that he hasn't struck out a ton of batters (50 K's). He has a long arm action, and his fastball, while hard, is straight and flat, so it can get hit. Hoeing is athletic with a clean delivery, but he's not deceptive for a hitter.

Gulliver Prep in the Miami area has had five players taken in the top five rounds, the most recent being infielder Adrian Marin, a third-rounder in 2012 currently in the Orioles organization. Del Castillo might be next on that list, though his bat-over-glove profile may keep him from reaching Marin's spot. Scouts universally like Del Castillo's swing from the left side of the plate. He recognizes pitches well, with a solid game plan, and should be able to hit for average. Because he doesn't expand the zone, he drives good hitters' pitches and has plenty of raw power to tap into. His work behind the plate remains behind what he can do in the batter's box. His arm is fringy, though it tends to be accurate, and his receiving skills will need continued refinement at the next level. If there was a need to move him out behind the plate, some feel left field would be the most suitable position for him. Del Castillo has a strong commitment to the University of Miami, so it might be tough to sign him beyond a certain round or bonus figure. Any team that makes a run at him will be buying the bat along with the hope that his defensive work improves so he can stay behind the plate, maximizing his value.

Guangorena made a name for himself on the summer showcase circuit, albeit with a different last name. He was Ojeda then, but changed it to Guangorena to honor his stepfather, who has been an integral part of the family. The name change did nothing to alter the California high schooler's evaluation as one of the most athletic catchers in the Draft class. A left-handed hitter, Guangorena can really impact the baseball, showing the ability to make consistent hard contact and the chance to hit for both average and power. He runs very well, especially for a catcher, with above-average run times down the line. Defensively, he has a plus arm and consistently shows excellent pop times. The rest of his defensive game, receiving and blocking, need work, but many feel his athleticism will help him improve in those areas in the future. High school catchers takes patience from a development standpoint, but Guangorena's offensive tools, arm and athleticism are bound to intrigue teams in the top five rounds. Any team considering taking him will have to convince him to forego his commitment to Cal State Fullerton.

As a 17-year-old with an athletic but thin build, Wrobleski stood out more with his projection than his present stuff for much of the spring. That profile usually lands a high school pitcher in college, but he generated some late helium when he showed improved velocity late in the season. He still might not be easy to lure away from Clemson, where he could get a chance to contribute as a two-way player. Wrobleski's fastball jumped from 88-92 mph to 91-93 mph as the Draft neared, with his low-three-quarters arm slot creating angle and sink. His slider showed more power and flashes of becoming a solid second offering as well. His fading changeup could be equally as effective. Wrobleski shows a willingness to use all three of his pitches, though he sometimes struggles to control them. He has the athleticism and delivery to figure out how to locate his offerings and remain a starter for the long term. His development will require time and patience if he turns pro this summer, though he has a high ceiling as a lefty with three potential solid or better pitches.

Williamson attracted next to no interest from pro or college teams as a Minnesota high school left-hander throwing in the low 80s. Two years later, he should be the first junior college southpaw selected and has a scholarship from Texas Christian as a backup plan. The consensus is that he's a better prospect than Daniel Tillo, whom the Royals drafted in the third round last year out of Iowa Western CC. Though Williamson still needs to develop further, the progress he has made at North Iowa Area CC is encouraging. He repeatedly has worked at 90-92 mph and topped out at 95 all spring despite brutal weather in the Midwest, and his fastball gets swings and misses in the strike zone with its late life and downhill plane. There's plenty of room to add strength to his 6-foot-5 frame, so he could continue to add velocity. Williamson's breaking ball lacks consistency and its shape sometime fluctuates, but he shows the potential for a solid slider. He needs to trust his changeup more often and flashes promising tumble when he does. He'll need to make some adjustments to his delivery to smooth out his control and command, but he has the athleticism to do so and his arm works well.

Projected first-round outfielder Jarred Kelenic (Waukesha West) isn't the only Wisconsin high school position player attracting scouts this spring. Campbell and third baseman Alex Binelas (Oak Creek) also would fit in the first five rounds on talent for some clubs, though they may not get popped early enough to divert from college. An Illinois recruit, Campbell is a better defender but has less power potential than Ben Rortvedt, a Badger State prep catcher whom the Twins drafted in the second round two years ago. Campbell has all the tools to succeed behind the plate. He receives and blocks well, and he makes accurate throws with solid arm strength. He plays with energy and has admirable leadership skills. Campbell has played through a hamate injury that has hampered him at the plate this spring. His strength stands out more than his bat speed, though his right-handed swing is quick enough. He currently employs a middle-away approach that's more conducive to hitting for average than power, though he could have more pop once he starts to turn on more pitches.

Becker shares much in common with Chandler Day, the last Ohio high school prospect with top-three-rounds talent. Both were ultraprojectable right-handers with Vanderbilt commitments, though Becker has better stuff at the same stage of their careers and may be more signable than Day, who slid to the Indians in 2015's 30th round. He helped his cause by turning in perhaps the best pitching performance at October's World Wood Bat Association World Championship, an overpowering two-inning stint. At his best, Becker can unleash 93-96 mph fastballs with armside run. He'll dip into the upper 80s at times, but he should add more velocity and consistency as he puts more muscle on his lanky 6-foot-6 frame. He can flash a plus curveball with 11-to-5 break and a solid changeup with some fade and tumble, though both pitches still need more refinement. For a tall teenage pitcher, Becker does a nice job of keeping his delivery in sync. He generally has good control and his command should improve as he gets stronger, though neither was as sharp as usual as he battled cold weather early this spring. If he does make it to the Commodores, it's easy to envision him becoming a first-round choice in 2021.

Part of a Team USA pitching staff that featured as many as seven potential 2018 first-rounders and won the 18-and-under World Cup last September, Marceaux may be the most advanced high school right-hander in this Draft. Despite his impressive polish, he has fringy fastball velocity and doesn't have a lot of projection remaining. That leads to concerns that he may not get picked quite high enough to sign him away from a Louisiana State commitment. Marceaux's best offering is an upper-70s curveball with 12-to-6 break. His fastball often parks at just 87-91 mph, though he can touch 94 and it plays above its velocity because he commands it so well and it features some sink and run. He has a deceptive changeup with fade and isn't afraid to use it, and he also can flash a solid low-80s slider at times. Marceaux has tremendous feel for all four of his pitches and for sequencing them to keep hitters off balance. There's very little effort in his delivery and he uses a high-three-quarters slot to compensate for his relatively small frame and create downhill angle. His moxie is an asset and another reason he could achieve more than his pure stuff might indicate.

Ethan Hankins and Kumar Rocker are the marquee names among Georgia high school pitchers, but a third Peach State prepster joined them on the Team USA pitching staff that won a gold medal at the 18-and-under World Cup last September. Wilcox also could join them in the first round of the 2018 Draft. Though he hasn't received the same notoriety, he might have a better likelihood of developing three solid pitches. Wilcox's fastball already sits at 91-94 mph and reaches 96 with heavy sink and the promise of more velocity once he fills out his athletic 6-foot-5 frame. His 81-84 mph changeup is equally effective, featuring fading action and often bottoming out at the plate. He'll also flash a plus slider in the low 80s, though it's not quite as consistent as his other two offerings. Wilcox repeats his mechanics well and has advanced feel for pitching. However, his delivery and arm action aren't as smooth as those of Hankins and Rocker, which could give pause to teams already leery of taking high school pitchers in the first round. If he follows through on his commitment to Georgia, he'll be Draft-eligible as a sophomore in 2020.

Florence (Ala.) High produced the seventh overall pick in the 2016 Draft in Braxton Garrett and has another quality arm two years later. Henry piqued the interest of scouts when he touched 97 mph and showed a plus curveball in his first outing of 2018, though he didn't maintain that stuff throughout the spring. He could go as high as the fourth round if teams believe that will be early enough to divert him from a Louisiana State commitment. Henry's fastball has ranged from 88-97 mph this year and sat at 92-93 toward the end of his season. His curveball can fluctuate between a slurve and a 12-to-6 breaker in the upper 70s with late bite. He shows some aptitude for throwing a changeup with armside life, though it can get a bit firm at times. While his stuff is inconsistent, Henry can flash two plus pitches and a solid third offering. Of greater concern is his delivery, which features effort and head whack and may make him a reliever in the long run. If he does attend college, he'll be Draft-eligible again as a sophomore in 2020.

There's always projectable high school pitching to be found in every Draft class. Left-handers who fit that description are a bit more rare, so when one pops up, he's bound to get a lot of attention. That's exactly what has transpired with Hansen, who talent-wise has top three round potential, but signability concerns have created some questions. Hansen has some pretty good now stuff, starting with a fastball he throws in the 88-92 mph range, and it's easy to dream on a lot more velocity as he fills out his 6-foot-4 frame. His changeup is his best secondary offering, with a chance to be at least above-average, thrown with good angle and downhill plane to both sides of the plate. He throws two types of three-quarter break curveballs, with the harder of the two possibly morphing into a slider in the future. He generally throws strikes and his overall command has improved this spring, though he still occasionally loses the strike zone for an inning or two. The biggest questions about Hansen are about what his future plans are. Originally committed to Stanford, Hansen switched gears and chose Vanderbilt as his college home. Beyond that, Hansen is a Mormon, with a potential mission looming, so any team considering taking him in the top few rounds would have to have a comfort level with what the southpaw wants to do.

Cecconi's summer started with a bang as he lit up radar guns at the Perfect Game National Showcase. He kept it up with a strong showing at USA Baseball's Tournament of Stars and made the 18U National Team's trial roster, though he did not make the final cut. And that was it, leaving scouts wishing they had seen more of the right-hander on the showcase circuit.
